body.title: The Efficacy of the Connect America Fund in Addressing US Internet Access Inequities
A Multifaceted Look at Starlink Performance
On the Mutual Dependency of Power Systems vs Information and Networking Technology

And now we are really excited to hear her talk. >> Uh thank you for the lovely introduction Durk. So let's get started. Okay again good morning everyone. My name is Harik Amenda and I'm going to be talking about our paper called the efficacy of the connect America fund and addressing US internet access inequities. This was a collaboration between my university UC Santa Barbara and UC Berkeley and this paper was previously published in scom of 2024 last year. So let's get started shall we? So presently there's a real prevalence of internet access inequity both in the United States as well as the entire world. In the United States alone, 30 million people lack access to high-speed or performant internet services with around 40% of these people being present in hard to serve rural or tribal areas. This is often because ISPs or internet service providers are not incentivized to build infrastructure in rural areas due to a low return on investment. More egregiously, even if people have access to an internet connection, around 10% of the population of the US still cannot afford this internet connection due to often high subscription costs. This has led to a situation where there's a real marginalization of underprivileged groups and populations in our digital society, which is becoming increasingly reliant on the internet for day-to-day activities such as applying for jobs or maybe even accessing medical records online. Even if we zoom out and look at the entire world, we can see when we're looking at the share of the world's offline population that a large number of these underprivileged groups are present in and around developing nations. That accounts for around 2.6 billion people that lack access to the internet. So what do we really do about this situation? Or more particularly, how can policymakers really influence internet access and make this situation better? That's why in the United States, Congress and the US government has historically instituted a series of multi-billion dollar policy interventions in order to improve the state of internet access for all. The program we're going to be talking about today is called the Connect America Fund or the CAF program. This started in 2011 and ran all the way for 2021 and dealt with around $10 billion of money. This worked by subsidizing new infrastructure by paying ISPs essentially in order to deploy infrastructure in rural areas that I was talking about earlier. However, there have been other initiatives such as the recent bead program that actually started in 2022 and it deals with four times the amount of money or $44 billion. Given the amount of funds being allocated to this, it's really important that we try and understand the fundamental problem in this situation. That is the relationship between ISPs and policy makers. You see, policymakers really don't know whether addresses are actually receiving service or not. That's why they rely on ISPs in order to provide self-reported data about which addresses actually receive service. You see, if you believe in the integrity of the process, which I'm sure many of you do, you might think that this situation is okay and we should believe in the integrity of the ISPs. However, unfortunately, that's not the reality. ISPs often admit to lying to the FCC about the size of their networks in order to block funding to the rivals. I want to bring you to your attention a lot of these newspaper headlines, including this particular one, including the state of Colorado, which is essentially a region of the United States, which was actually challenging 13,000 speed inaccuracies in the new federal broadband map that, you guessed it, relied on ISP self-reported data. So, what do we really do about this situation? Or more particularly, how do we really reduce our our reliance on self-reported data from ISPs about internet access? In order to do this, our team at UCSB decided to leverage our prior work that was published in SIGCOM of 2023. This was based around the broadband plan quering tool or the BQT tool. Essentially, the BQT tool allowed us to scalably measure and see if we could interact with ISP websites and extract three dimensions of information about addresses. These three dimensions are internet availability, quality, and affordability. This is the first time we're able to access fine-grained information about broadband and their advertised speeds. The BQT tool is easy to use and allows us to extract CSVs such as this, which gives us the time for quering the address, the service status, as well as fine grain pricing information along with the download speed and upload speed. So for example, if we run the BQT tool, we might get a response such as this for a particular address which I have blurred for privacy reasons. We can see that this address is actually receiving 768 kbps or is also required to pay around $55 a month. We saw that many of these addresses usually only had one plan to select from. And in the case when the addresses had no option, our BQT tool really usually received a response such as this where there was no service available to pay for. Now, how does the BQT tool work in the first place? Well, I want to show you that anyone can use it. Essentially given a list of addresses which are usually residential in nature you can pair that with selenium code from regarding an ISP essentially together these are deployed in a docker container interface or a cluster. We also make the use of a VPN proxy. This VPN proxy is usually used to bypass antibbot detection services that many ISPs usually employ because they would not like people to collect this data. Usually these these VPN services were actually provided to us by the bright initiative. So we sincerely thank them for access to that. So finally using our Docker container interface were able to scal scalably collect surface ability and quality and pricing information and store it locally and securely at our UCSB servers. So now that we finally have our BQT tool, how can we really use it in order to assess the Connect America Fund or the CAF program's true success? But let's not get ahead of ourselves. Let me explain and introduce what the calf program is about in the first place. So essentially the goal of the calf program was to address the real disparity in internet access which was affordable and performant in high-cost rural regions. As I explained earlier, these regions often do not receive anywhere comparable internet speeds to urban areas. And how it worked is essentially the calf program allowed the FCC or the Federal Communications Commission governmental body of the United States to subsidize ISPs to build infrastructure in these regions that lack broadband internet access. The phase one of the calf program actually consisted of 6 million addresses scattered throughout the United States. And the FCC also specified a set of service quality agreements that ISPs were required to adhere to. This was actually a 10 megabit per second download speed and 1 megabit per second upload speed. And also they required to adhere to specific price requirements so that consumers were not overcharged. And finally, as for the certification process, as I was talking about earlier, ISPs actually certify these addresses through a self-reported framework that is actually not essentially public in nature. In order to in order to impress upon you the scale of the calf program, you can see this map of all 8 million addresses which were part of phase two of the program. You can see there are regions all around the United States. And in its infancy, even the calf program was extended among 37 states in the United States. So now that we know about the calf program, how would we really investigate how effective it was in really in expanding internet access given that it dealt with such a large scale of funds and ran for approximately 11 years? In order to do this came up with a set of key policy questions. The questions include as following. Question one is are ISPs always truthful about the addresses that they certify is served that is the 6 million addresses of the calf program. Question two was are these ISPs always compliant with the FCC service quality and rate requirements that is the 10 megabit per second download speed and the 1 megabit per second upload speed. Finally, and most importantly, the major question was, are these regulated monopolies that the ISPs are essentially creating using the calf program really offering a better value to users than the unregulated ones that would have been in place if the calf program was not present? So in order to do this using our BQT tool, we decided to create a data set to essentially do an applesto apples comparison between our certified self-reported data set that was provided by ISPs versus the advertised information that we're able to extract using our BQT tool. However, we ran into a problem where 6 million addresses was quite a lot to query and we didn't know which ISPs or which addresses were the most important. In order to figure out figure out this information, we did an analysis where we f found that a lot of a couple of ISPs were essentially monopolies and operating and were in charge of essentially more than 50% of the calf addresses. These top three ISPs were AT&T, Century Link, and Frontier Internet. We also decided to study a smaller ISP such as Consolidated Internet to see how they operate in relation to these big conglomerates. We also decided to select 15 states for geographic diversity and we used a robust sampling strategy where we sampled at least 10% of the addresses from each of these census block groups. Given this robust sampling strategy, over the course of a couple months, our team was able to create our the first of its kind fine grain data set of 687,000 street addresses with 500k of these being approximately calf addresses with fine grain download upload speed and pricing information. So now that we finally have this data set, we can start to answer the key policy questions that I was talking about earlier. Let's see what we h what happens. So the first question was are ISPs really truthful about the addresses that they certify as served? This is some ISPs by the way. After collecting data using our BQT tool, we saw that unfortunately there was an overall serviceability rate of only 55%. This is actually shocking because ISPs had certified that they served 100% of the addresses and they had received funds for every single one of these addresses part of the calf program. And when we desegregated this number based on ISP, we saw that AT&T had a pretty low serviceability rate of only around 31%. That is only one in three addresses that AT&T was servicing as part of the calf program actually received or had the chance to buy internet service in the first place. Given that there was such a divergence between AT&T and their and the other ISPs, we decided to investigate a little further. We saw that AT&T received around $1 billion or 10% of the total calf funding as a single ISP. When we desegregated these numbers based on states, we saw that several states actually received less than 31% of serviceability rates. One state that I want to highlight in particular was Georgia. We actually wanted to see where these serviced addresses stacked up in comparison to the non-serviceable addresses geospatially. Did they all were they all in the same place or were they clustered? As we had thought unfortunately we could see through this geo geospatial map this is essentially a heat map where the dark blue regions indicate high serviceable regions where around 100% of these addresses were serviced and these red regions unfortunately indicate addresses which and census blocks that received little to no service. We can see that the high serviceability regions were concentrated in and around urban areas. This is a metropolitan city in the USA called Atlanta which has a significant population and pretty good internet access already. And when we move to the remote rural regions, unfortunately we can see that the internet service drops drastically. This is particularly disheartening because AT&T actually ignored the primary targets for the calf program at least in the state of Georgia. These were remote rural regions that were actually the reason for the allocation of the funding in the first place. So now that we answered the first question, let's answer the second question and see if there's any difference in our results when we take in speed into consideration. That is, are ISPs really compliant with the speed and price requirements, the 10 megabit per second download and the 1 megabit per second upload? And fortunately, we actually observed an aggregate compliance rate of only 33%. That is out of all of the addresses we sampled, only one in three addresses actually received the chance to buy service. and buy service that was greater than 10 megabit per second download and one megabit per second upload. In particular, when we disagregated this based on ISP, we saw that AT&T and Frontier Internet were the worst offenders with actually all ISPs offering substandard broadband plans. We saw at a lot of AT&T addresses which received service were only receiving less than 1 megabit per second around 768 kbps. This is particularly egregious because in this digital world world with dynamic websites and streaming videos, it's extremely hard to perform any sort of internet activity with that sort of connectivity. And in particular, given the drastic difference between serviceability and compliance between Frontier, we decided to investigate. We actually saw that a lot of users could not even choose any specific speed tier and that even if users paid for a broadband plan, they could not guarantee any particular level of performance whatsoever and they were in violation of the 10 megabit per second download speed and the 1 megabit per second upload speed. So finally, now that we answered our first two questions, we can start to answer our third question with respect to non-caf addresses. That is, are the regulated monopolies that the the connect America program is essentially creating really better than the addresses when there was no connect program in the first place? In order to do this, we decided to focus on seven states given our large scale of data collection. And we decided to get our non-f residential data from a data set provided with our partnership with Zillow. We also made sure to remove all addresses which actually received no service as we wanted to measure and compare the speed between calf addresses and non-caf addresses. This is really important. So what we found was actually only in 27% of our observed census block groups did the calf program actually have a real benefit and the users received better speed tiers than non-calf addresses. When we decide to look into this 27% of census block groups, we did see something a little bit better. We plotted a CDF showing our percentage increase in average maximum speed for the calf program. And when we look for 25% of the addresses, you can see that they had a percentage increase of more than 200 Mbps. This is quite significant for the user and show that when the calf program was actually implemented correctly, it had a real improvement for the end user. So now I would like to talk about competitive addresses. So as we all know competition usually increases subscription rates. So we wanted to confirm this and we actually plotted a distribution showing the scale of download speeds for addresses that were part of the calf program and addresses that were not part of the part of the calf program but were also surrounded by competitive ISPs. The ISPs we used for competition in our cases were usually Exfinity uh Comcast as well as Spectrum in our case. What we saw was that when we look at the top 25% of these addresses until the 60th percentile, it's usually the same. But when we look at the 20%, you can see that the calf addresses around competition addresses were essentially receiving better than 90 Mbps. This is quite significant. And to show you this information spatially, I'm show if you I if I bring your attention to the right picture, you can see two block groups. One block group is really close to the other one and they're essentially in the same area of the United States that is Georgia. They're both serviced by Century Link Internet which is a calf ISP. However, there's an important difference. One block group is essentially a monopoly with Centry Link serving all the addresses and the users having no other chance to apply for a different ISP. Whereas the other block group has Centry Link as well as a competing ISP right in the vicinity. And we see where there is competition, there's a humongous increase in the download speeds with often more than six times in this case. So what did we learn from all this? And let's try and revisit our questions and break down whether we actually have our answers. So our first question of course was are ISPs really truthful about the addresses that they certify is served? And unfortunately no. only 55% of the addresses we actually surveyed received real service from the ISP that they had funds for. And question two was, are these ISPs always compliant with the FCC service quality and rate requirements? And unfortunately, we saw no. The overall compliance rate for our service quality was only around 33%. And for question three, well, I wish I had better news for you, but unfortunately the regulated monopolies did offer a better value for users. However, it was inconsistent. It was only in 27% of the cases that we saw. So, what do we do with all this information? Well, I would like to impress upon you this was actually at the time of its publication at least one of the first largescale analyses of multi-billion dollar broadband programs involving ISPs and their certifications. And I'm really calling for a post hawk and more thorough evaluation framework in order to certify the ISPs are really compliant when receiving money. And finally, I wish the process of the certification is more public and transparent for the benefit of policy makers as well as the end users who are the real ones suffering. Finally, as for current and future efforts, we're currently working to expand our broadband plan quering tool or BQT tool for short to more ISPs. We're also working with other state and local government organizations such as the state of Virginia as well as California, which are all regions of the United States. We're also working to assess other policy interventions as well, and we're working with other research institutions such as Pew Research and the ILRS. We've received a lot of press coverage, including a Harvard Law Review blog, which looks at this from a more policybased perspective, as well as the Internet Society blog post. We've also submitted a US Supreme Court brief in support against Wisconsin Bell, which is essentially a sub subsidiary of AT&T. the result of the Supreme Court be brief came out and essentially uh the Supreme Court you ruled unanimously in favor against uh the subsidiary of AT&T essentially. So thank you all for listening. I extremely appreciate it. My name is Haramanda. You can find me and email me about any questions at hmanda@usb.edu. We also do numerous studies like this at UC Santa Barbara and you can find our website at address.cs.usb.edu edu and you can even scan the QR code link. Thank you for listening.
